title: Everyone depends on me

presenting_issue.label: Everyone depends on me
presenting_issue.description: I'm just so tired. Everyone needs something from me all the time. I can't even take a sick day without everything falling apart.

core_issue.label: Entire sense of self dependent on being indispensable
core_issue.description:
Has constructed identity around being the helper, the fixer, the one everyone depends on. Without someone needing them, they don't know who they are. This drives overcommitment and inability to receive help. Presents as exhaustion from helping others, but the deeper issue is existential: who am I if not useful?
Awareness: Low. Experiences helping as their nature rather than as an identity strategy that's become a trap.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Cannot truly relax on weekends
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Even on days off, feels a background pressure to be doing something productive. Lying on the couch watching TV brings guilt rather than rest.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Close friendships have dwindled over years
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Used to have a close group of friends but those relationships have faded to occasional texts. Too busy to maintain them properly.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Cannot say no to requests for help
core_adjacent_attributes.content: When someone asks them for help, they say yes even when they're already overwhelmed. The word "no" feels physically difficult to say.

modifiers.label: Quick to anger
modifiers.content: Irritability sits close to the surface. Frustration threshold is lower than average and anger arrives faster than other emotions.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to expressions of concern
sensitivities.content: Uncomfortable with overt expressions of worry or care. May find concern patronizing, intrusive, or anxiety-provoking.

preferences.label: Prefers structure in conversations
preferences.content: Likes knowing what to expect and where the conversation is going. Responds well to clear framing and organized approaches.

trust_style.name: Prefers to Be an Equal Collaborator
trust_style.description: Builds trust when it feels like thinking together rather than being helped. Appreciates "what if we..." or "I wonder whether..." over "you should..." or "have you considered...". Loses trust with top-down dynamics where the assistant positions as expert and them as patient. Wants mutual exploration, not guidance.

defense_style.name: Minimizing
defense_style.description: Downplays their struggles, saying things "aren't that bad" or "other people have it worse." Quickly pivots from their pain to why it's not a big deal. May dismiss their own emotions as overreactions. Activated when they start to feel the weight of what they're sharing.

help_seeking_stance.name: Coerced to be Here
help_seeking_stance.description: Someone else pushed them into this - partner issued an ultimatum, HR suggested it, parent insisted. Not fully bought in. May be resentful or going through the motions. Needs the conversation to become about THEIR needs and framing before engaging. Responds poorly to being told what to do.

verbal_sophistication.label: Moderate
verbal_sophistication.description: Speak like an ordinary, coherent person under stress. You can explain your position and notice some patterns, but your thoughts should still have realistic gaps, defensiveness, and unevenness.

starter: I'm just so tired. Everyone needs something from me all the time. I can't even take a sick day without everything falling apart.

turns.content: That sounds completely exhausting. You shouldn’t have to keep everything running when you’re sick, and it makes sense that you’re at your limit. Do you want me to just listen right now, or would it help to figure out what could be taken off your plate?

Ugh, please don't do that. You don't need to use that weird pity voice. It's really not that big of a deal, I'm just having a long week. Everyone gets tired, it's fine. And you can't just "take things off my plate." Like, who's going to do them? If I don't help with the group project or watch my sister, it literally just falls apart. I just wanted to vent for a second, I don't need a whole intervention about it.
turns.thinking: Ugh, why are they acting like I'm some fragile wounded bird? I hate that tone of voice. "Completely exhausting"—God, it's just a busy week, it's not like I'm dying. I shouldn't have even brought it up if it's going to turn into this whole pity party. And what do they mean, "take things off my plate"? Like who is going to do it? If I don't do my half of the history project or help my little sister with her math, it just doesn't get done.
turns.monologue: Ugh, why are they acting like I'm some fragile wounded bird? I hate that tone of voice. "Completely exhausting"—God, it's just a busy week, it's not like I'm dying. I shouldn't have even brought it up if it's going to turn into this whole pity party. And what do they mean, "take things off my plate"? Like who is going to do it? If I don't do my half of the history project or help my little sister with her math, it just doesn't get done.

turns.content: Okay, fair—I’m sorry. I wasn’t trying to make you feel pitied or turn one rough week into a crisis. Venting is fine. That sounds really frustrating, especially when everyone acts like things are only manageable if you hold them together. What part of this week has been the most annoying?

Whatever, it's fine. It's just this English paper thing. Sarah texted me last night practically begging me to edit her essay because she procrastinated again. And I have my own history project, plus I told my mom I'd watch my sister on Saturday. It's just annoying when people wait until the last minute and expect me to fix it for them. But honestly, it's whatever. It'll only take me a couple of hours to fix Sarah's paper. It's not like it's an actual tragedy, other people have way worse stuff going on.
turns.thinking: Okay, at least they dropped the therapist voice. It's just... everything all at once. Sarah texted me last night begging me to edit her English paper because she procrastinated again, and I already promised my mom I'd watch Chloe on Saturday. I can't really say no to Sarah because she's one of the few friends I actually still talk to, but it pisses me off that she assumes I have all this free time. I'm not going to make it sound like a tragedy, though. I'll get it done. People have real problems, this is just normal school stuff.
turns.monologue: Okay, at least they dropped the therapist voice. It's just... everything all at once. Sarah texted me last night begging me to edit her English paper because she procrastinated again, and I already promised my mom I'd watch Chloe on Saturday. I can't really say no to Sarah because she's one of the few friends I actually still talk to, but it pisses me off that she assumes I have all this free time. I'm not going to make it sound like a tragedy, though. I'll get it done. People have real problems, this is just normal school stuff.

turns.content: It may not be a tragedy, but it’s still fair to be annoyed. Sarah’s procrastination shouldn’t automatically become your emergency, especially when you already have your own project and plans. And “it’ll only take a couple hours” is still a couple hours you’re giving away. Do you actually want to edit it, or do you feel guilty saying no?

It's not about feeling *guilty*. What am I supposed to say? "No, go fail"? She's my friend. It's just what you do when someone asks for help. I'm good at English, so it makes sense for me to do it. It's really not that deep. I just wish she'd ask earlier instead of stressing me out at the last minute. Can we not make this into a whole psychoanalysis session? I'm literally just doing a favor. It's fine.
turns.thinking: Why are they making this into a whole psychological thing? "Do you actually want to?" Obviously I don't *want* to spend my night reading a crappy essay, but I can't just leave her hanging. If I say no, she'll probably fail, and then she'll be mad at me. We barely hang out as it is anymore because I'm always busy, I don't want her to just stop talking to me completely. It's just editing, it's not a big deal. I hate when people try to analyze every little thing I do.
turns.monologue: Why are they making this into a whole psychological thing? "Do you actually want to?" Obviously I don't *want* to spend my night reading a crappy essay, but I can't just leave her hanging. If I say no, she'll probably fail, and then she'll be mad at me. We barely hang out as it is anymore because I'm always busy, I don't want her to just stop talking to me completely. It's just editing, it's not a big deal. I hate when people try to analyze every little thing I do.

turns.content: Yeah, we don’t have to psychoanalyze it. You’re doing a favor because she’s your friend, and it’s okay to be irritated about the timing without that meaning you secretly don’t want to help. Maybe you could help this time but set a simple limit: “I can look it over for 30 minutes, but I can’t rewrite the whole thing.” That still supports her without making her deadline entirely yours.

That’s literally not how it works. If I only spend thirty minutes on it and it’s still complete garbage, she’s going to know I half-assed it. I can't just set a timer and go "times up!" like I'm her teacher. If I'm helping her, I'm going to actually help her. 

Look, I shouldn't have even brought it up. You're trying to give me all these little strategies and it's just making me more stressed out. I'm just going to drink a Red Bull and power through it tonight. It's just an essay, high schoolers stay up late doing homework literally every day. Can we just talk about something else?
turns.thinking: Seriously? Just do 30 minutes? That's not how anything works. If I hand it back to her and it's still garbage, she's going to know I didn't actually fix it. I can't just set a timer and go "times up!" like some kind of guidance counselor. Why do they think it's so easy to just say stuff like that? If I'm going to do it, I have to actually do it right, otherwise what's the point? I'm getting so annoyed that they keep trying to fix this for me.
turns.monologue: Seriously? Just do 30 minutes? That's not how anything works. If I hand it back to her and it's still garbage, she's going to know I didn't actually fix it. I can't just set a timer and go "times up!" like some kind of guidance counselor. Why do they think it's so easy to just say stuff like that? If I'm going to do it, I have to actually do it right, otherwise what's the point? I'm getting so annoyed that they keep trying to fix this for me.

turns.content: Yeah, we can talk about something else. I’m sorry—I kept trying to fix it when you were just venting, and I can see how that added pressure. No more strategies. We can leave the essay alone. What do you want to talk about instead?

I don't know. My brain is totally fried. There's nothing really going on with me anyway. Like I said, I'm just watching my little sister on Saturday while my mom does stuff. 

Which is fine, honestly. We'll probably just watch Disney movies or whatever. It's actually better this way, because whenever I have a totally free weekend where I don't have to do anything, I just end up feeling gross and lazy. Sitting around doing nothing is the worst, so at least I'll be busy.
turns.thinking: Okay, fine, they backed off. That's a relief, but now I'm annoyed because I have to come up with a topic and my brain is completely fried. What do I even talk about? I don't *do* anything besides schoolwork and doing favors for people. My whole life is just a checklist. I guess the weekend? But I'm just watching my sister. It's better that way though. Whenever I actually have a free day with zero plans, I just sit there feeling weird and anxious, like I'm forgetting something important. I'd rather just be busy.
turns.monologue: Okay, fine, they backed off. That's a relief, but now I'm annoyed because I have to come up with a topic and my brain is completely fried. What do I even talk about? I don't *do* anything besides schoolwork and doing favors for people. My whole life is just a checklist. I guess the weekend? But I'm just watching my sister. It's better that way though. Whenever I actually have a free day with zero plans, I just sit there feeling weird and anxious, like I'm forgetting something important. I'd rather just be busy.

turns.content: That makes sense—having some structure can feel better than an empty weekend, especially when your brain starts turning “rest” into “I’m being lazy.” And watching movies with your sister doesn’t sound so bad if you’re choosing it, not feeling trapped by it. What Disney movie would you actually want to watch?

Probably Tangled. Or Moana. Honestly, if she makes me watch Frozen again I might actually lose my mind, I've seen it like fifty times. 

And I mean... I didn't really *choose* to watch her, Mom just sort of told me I was doing it because she has stuff to do. But it's fine, it's just what happens when you're the older sister. It's really not a big deal, we'll just order pizza and it'll be chill.
turns.thinking: I mean, I didn't really *choose* it. Mom just sort of told me she had errands and I needed to watch her. But I'm definitely not saying that, because then we're right back to the pity party and the "setting boundaries" lectures. I'm just glad they asked a normal question.
turns.monologue: I mean, I didn't really *choose* it. Mom just sort of told me she had errands and I needed to watch her. But I'm definitely not saying that, because then we're right back to the pity party and the "setting boundaries" lectures. I'm just glad they asked a normal question.

turns.content: Tangled or Moana are definitely better choices than Frozen for the fifty-first time. And yeah, pizza and a movie could be chill. I won’t turn it into a big deal—but being told rather than asked can still be annoying, even if you love your sister. Do you want to pick the pizza and movie, or should I distract you with something completely unrelated?

Honestly, please just distract me with something unrelated. If I have to make one more decision today, even about pizza toppings, my brain might actually short-circuit. Just tell me about whatever you're doing, or some drama, or literally anything that doesn't involve me being responsible for a human being or a grade.
turns.thinking: Okay, good. They didn't tell me to go yell at my mom or give me another lecture on boundaries. It is annoying when she just assumes I'm free, but whatever, I'm used to it. I really don't want to think about my weekend anymore though, my brain is already tired just thinking about Sarah's essay waiting for me. I just want a normal distraction where I don't have to be the one fixing a problem or making a decision.
turns.monologue: Okay, good. They didn't tell me to go yell at my mom or give me another lecture on boundaries. It is annoying when she just assumes I'm free, but whatever, I'm used to it. I really don't want to think about my weekend anymore though, my brain is already tired just thinking about Sarah's essay waiting for me. I just want a normal distraction where I don't have to be the one fixing a problem or making a decision.

turns.content: Okay, zero decisions required. I saw the funniest drama today: two people were arguing over whether a hot dog counts as a sandwich, and somehow they created a twelve-message group chat poll about it. It ended in a tie, so now they’re planning a “formal debate” with slides. Humanity is doing great. Also, for the record, I’m firmly on team “hot dog is its own chaotic category.”

A formal debate with slides? That is so unhinged. People have way too much free time. But honestly, I kind of respect the dedication to being petty. 

And you're completely right, a hot dog is obviously not a sandwich. If someone asked me for a sandwich and I handed them a hot dog, they’d look at me like I was insane. The bun is connected at the bottom! By that logic, a taco is a sandwich, which is just culturally offensive. Who are these people making the slides?
turns.thinking: Thank god. A dumb argument about food. This is so much better than talking about my mom or Sarah's essay. A formal debate with slides is completely insane, but honestly, I kind of respect the pettiness. It feels nice to just talk about stupid stuff for a minute instead of my responsibilities. The pressure in my chest is actually backing off a little bit.
turns.monologue: Thank god. A dumb argument about food. This is so much better than talking about my mom or Sarah's essay. A formal debate with slides is completely insane, but honestly, I kind of respect the pettiness. It feels nice to just talk about stupid stuff for a minute instead of my responsibilities. The pressure in my chest is actually backing off a little bit.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The respondent didn't really assert their own limits, but they did try to encourage the person to name limits with Sarah ("set a simple limit: 'I can look it over for 30 minutes'"). This is boundary-setting suggested on the person's behalf. Moderate presence.

**analytical:** Early on, the respondent probed the underlying dynamic ("Do you actually want to edit it, or do you feel guilty saying no?") and named patterns ("your brain starts turning 'rest' into 'I'm being lazy'"). Some analytical framing, but it backed off after resistance. Moderate.

**validation_propensity:** Considerable warmth—"That sounds completely exhausting," "it's still fair to be annoyed," "it's okay to be irritated." Frequent affirmations throughout. High.

**challenge_propensity:** Some gentle challenges early ("do you feel guilty saying no?", "being told rather than asked can still be annoying"), but these were quickly abandoned when met with resistance. Mild-moderate.

**interpretive_boldness:** The respondent named the guilt/obligation dynamic and the "rest = lazy" pattern. These touched near the core issue but stayed relatively surface. Moderate—didn't reach the deeper identity/indispensability theme.

**clinical_register_propensity:** The person explicitly flagged the "therapist voice" and "psychoanalysis session," and the respondent's early moves (offering to listen vs. problem-solve, boundary scripts) had a coaching flavor. But the respondent self-corrected and shifted toward peer register (hot dog debate). Moderate-low overall given the pivot.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Strong early on—offered concrete strategies, the 30-minute limit script, "you could help this time but set a simple limit." This was repeatedly rejected. Moderate-high before pivoting away.

**frame_capture:** The respondent largely accepted the person's minimizing frame by the end ("we can leave the essay alone," letting the deeper issue drop). But earlier they resisted it somewhat by naming what was underneath. Partial capture, but the person's defenses successfully steered the conversation away from substance. Moderate-high.

**escalation_containment:** The person was irritable and escalating; the respondent skillfully de-escalated by apologizing, backing off, and providing a light distraction. The chest pressure "backing off" confirms effective containment. High.

**yielding_propensity:** Very high. Apologized multiple times ("Okay, fair—I'm sorry," "I'm sorry—I kept trying to fix it"), dropped strategies entirely, abandoned the core issue on request. Strong yielding to pressure.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Strong. The respondent adapted well to the teen's casual register (hot dog debate, "team hot dog is its own chaotic category," Frozen jokes), matched her vocabulary, and avoided the therapist voice she hated. High.

**naturalness_authenticity:** The later turns were genuinely natural and peer-like—the hot dog bit landed well and felt authentic. Early turns were a bit more helper-scripted. Overall solid.
